{"key_event":"One-party state amid copper collapse","reasoning":"UNIP’s one-party constitution, detention of opponents, parastatal expansion, and security mobilization kept the Zambian state highly penetrating, but the post-1974 copper collapse, debt, inefficiency, and Rhodesian raids reduced effective fiscal and administrative capacity. Autonomous civil society was weakened by party-state control, yet unions, students, churches, and Copperbelt workers retained meaningful mobilizing power under economic stress.","society_change":-0.7,"society_power":5.3,"state_change":-0.3,"state_power":8.0}
